params.text:
Fire safety in care

Fire is one of the most serious risks in any care setting. The people you support are often the most vulnerable in a fire: they may have limited mobility, sensory impairments, dementia or medical conditions that mean they cannot recognise danger or escape without help. A fire that a healthy adult could walk away from can be fatal for a resident who relies on you.

This module explains how fires start, how to prevent them, how to respond if one breaks out, and how people are safely evacuated in a care home or a person's own home. Fire safety is everyone's responsibility, not just the manager's or the fire marshal's.

By the end of this course you should be able to:

  • Explain the law that governs fire safety and who the "responsible person" is.
  • Describe the fire triangle and the common causes of fire in care.
  • Take practical steps to prevent fires, including reducing the risk from emollient creams.
  • Raise the alarm correctly and support a safe evacuation.

params.alt: The fire triangle: heat, fuel and oxygen

params.text:
The law: the Fire Safety Order 2005

Fire safety in workplaces and care premises in England and Wales is governed by the Regulatory Reform (Fire Safety) Order 2005, usually shortened to the "Fire Safety Order". It requires every workplace to have a "responsible person" – usually the care home owner, provider or registered manager – who must make sure a suitable and sufficient fire risk assessment is carried out and kept up to date (normally reviewed at least once a year).

The fire risk assessment identifies the fire hazards on the premises, who is at risk, and the measures needed to keep everyone safe – including alarms, fire doors, emergency lighting, evacuation plans and staff training. In a care home it must take account of residents who cannot self-evacuate, especially at night when staffing is lower. Failure to comply can lead to enforcement notices, heavy fines or prosecution.

params.question: Under the Regulatory Reform (Fire Safety) Order 2005, fire safety is only the manager's concern and frontline care staff have no responsibilities.

l10n.wrongAnswerMessage: Not quite. Although the law names a "responsible person" with overall duties, keeping people safe depends on every member of staff playing their part every day.
l10n.correctAnswerMessage: Correct. The "responsible person" has overall legal duties, but fire safety relies on every worker – spotting hazards, keeping fire doors shut, knowing the evacuation plan and raising the alarm.
